pattern: XOR has no notion of "smaller partial result is always better", so a path DP must track every achievable running XOR at each cell rather than a single best value -- and because every cell value is small, the number of distinct running totals stays small too.
hints: Unlike a sum, a smaller partial XOR doesn't guarantee a smaller final XOR, so a plain shortest-path DP that only keeps the best value per cell will miss the true optimum.

Instead, at every cell keep the full set of XOR totals reachable by some path to that cell, built from the union of the sets reachable from above and from the left, each XORed with the current cell's value.

Because every value is bounded to a small number of bits, that reachable set can never grow beyond that many distinct totals per cell, so the whole grid stays cheap to process despite the exponential number of raw paths.

edge_inputs.description: single row, only one path exists
single column, only one path exists
identical values everywhere, XOR cancels along even-length paths
zeros surrounding a single nonzero cell not on every path
cell values at the maximum allowed magnitude

stress.description: roughly square grid near the maximum cell budget
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    side = max(1, min(size, 31))
    grid = [[rng.randint(0, 1023) for _ in range(side)] for _ in range(side)]
    return [grid]

stress.description: a single very long row exercising the path-count edge
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    grid = [[rng.randint(0, 1023) for _ in range(n)]]
    return [grid]
